title: Chirality-sensitive mobility and dissipation of Brownian motion on a helical landscape
abstract: We study the Brownian dynamics and linear response of a particle with inertia moving in a 2-dimensional helical landscape imprinted on a cylindrical surface. In the harmonic well approximation, the deterministic motion separates into free propagation along the screw direction and harmonic motion in the transverse screw-normal direction. We show that for isotropic damping this simplification survives in the Langevin description, whereas anisotropic damping along the axial and angular directions couples the stochastic dynamics and destroys separability. The resulting anisotropic model is formulated as a linear Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process in phase space with a zero mode associated with diffusion along the screw coordinate, so that in an infinite system the full phase-space dynamics does not relax to a stationary distribution. To treat transport in this setting, we construct the stationary dynamics in the stable subspace obtained after projecting out the zero mode. This leads to a linear response theory for this system and yields closed analytical expressions for stationary time-correlation functions and the dynamical mobility tensor in both the time and frequency domains. The off-diagonal elements of the mobility tensor describe cross-response between axial forcing and angular motion, and between applied torque and axial transport. Consistent with time reversal symmetry, these cross mobilities are equal and provide a direct dynamical signature of the helical geometry. In addition, a simultaneous application of driving in both the axial and angular direction reveals asymmetry in energy dissipation rate due the helical landscape.
summary: This paper provides a complete analytical description of how a helical landscape affects the transport and dissipation of a Brownian particle. It demonstrates that the geometry of the landscape creates a direct link between axial and angular motion, which can be detected through cross-mobility and asymmetric energy absorption.
main_problem: Investigating how the helical geometry and damping anisotropy of a landscape influence the Brownian dynamics, mobility, and energy dissipation of an inertial particle.
main_result: The authors derive analytical expressions for the mobility tensor and show that chirality induces cross-response between axial and angular motion, as well as an asymmetry in energy dissipation.
method: The study employs Langevin dynamics, linear response theory, and a projection operator approach to separate the dynamics into a stable subspace and a diffusive zero-mode.
model_or_system: A classical Brownian particle with inertia moving in a 2D helical potential on a cylindrical surface, modeled using a harmonic well approximation and Langevin equations.
key_observables: Dynamical mobility tensor (diagonal and off-diagonal elements), momentum time-correlation functions, and energy dissipation rate.
important_parameters: Helix handedness (chirality), damping anisotropy, radius-to-pitch ratio, and temperature.
main_assumptions: The potential is approximated as a harmonic well, the particle is structureless, and the system is treated within the framework of Markovian Langevin dynamics.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the helical potential landscape; Figure 4 shows momentum correlations; Figure 5 presents Nyquist plots of AC mobility; Figure 6 shows parametric variation of DC mobilities; Figure 7 shows energy dissipation absorption spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the helical model, develops the stochastic framework using coordinate transformations, analyzes the isotropic and anisotropic damping regimes, derives the mobility tensor via linear response theory, and concludes with energy dissipation analysis.
why_interesting: The derivation of transport coefficients and dissipation in a non-stationary, non-equilibrium setting with broken symmetry is highly relevant to researchers studying open quantum systems and driven-dissipative dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
